coredata.dc:title: Sustainable-biomass-derived multifunctional carbon dots as fluorescent probes for multi-purpose advanced imaging, migration and security solutions

coredata.dc:description: Multifunctional tunable-emissive carbon dots (ME-CDs) were hydrothermally synthesized and characterized for their structural, morphological, and optical properties, as well as their applications in biological imaging and anti-counterfeiting. Scanning electron microscopy revealed uniformly dispersed wrinkle-shaped particles, while high-resolution transmission electron microscopy confirmed sizes below 5 nm. Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy validated the carbon-dominant composition, with functional groups essential for biological interactions. Raman spectroscopy showed a partially disordered graphite-like structure with a 0.72 D/G intensity ratio, and Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy identified surface functionalities. The UV–vis absorption spectrum exhibited characteristic π–π* and n–π* transitions at 280 nm and 340 nm, while fluorescence studies demonstrated strong excitation-dependent emission suitable for imaging. ME-CDs showed remarkable stability, maintaining fluorescence intensity over 200 days and under UV irradiation. As fluorescent ink, they displayed clear visibility under UV light and prolonged durability. Biocompatibility studies confirmed non-toxicity, enabling effective labeling of fibroblasts and human mesenchymal stem cells (HMSCs) with confocal visualization. ME-CDs also penetrated 3D HMSC spheroids, providing multicolor fluorescence without affecting cell viability, and efficiently tracked macrophage migration. The robust optical properties, biocompatibility, and durability of ME-CDs underscore their potential for long-term applications in bioimaging and anti-counterfeiting.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Portraying the synthesis of ME-CDs from black mulberry (Morus nigra) fruit through an economical one-step hydrothermal path.
Scanning electron microscopy image of synthesized ME-CDs with different magnifications.

Elemental mapping of synthesized ME-CDs. (a) Scanning electron microscopy image; (b) considered a significant element in parenteral components, carbon is homogeneously distributed; (c and d) oxygen and nitrogen are localized in specific areas within the carbon matrix.
(a–c) TEM images of the synthesized ME-CDs and (d) high-resolution TEM image of a single ME-CD.

(a) XRD pattern of the synthesized ME-CDs, (b) Raman spectrum of the synthesized ME-CDs, (c) FTIR spectrum of the synthesized ME-CDs, and (d) XPS total spectrum of the synthesized ME-CDs (inset: elemental composition chart of ME-CDs).
XPS spectra of the synthesized ME-CDs: (a) deconvolution of C 1 s enlarged region, (b) deconvolution of O 1 s enlarged region, and (c) deconvolution of N 1 s enlarged region.

The optical spectra of the synthesized ME-CDs: (a) UV−vis absorption spectrum (inset showed the photographs of aqueous ME-CDs in daylight and under UV light), (b) fluorescence emission and excitation spectra, (c) fluorescence emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths, and (d) the normalized fluorescence spectra with different excitation wavelengths.

(a) The fluorescence intensity changes of synthesized ME-CDs during several days (day 1 and day 200); inset: Digital photographs of fresh and several months stored ME-CDs aqueous suspension under normal light and UV light and (b) the fluorescence intensity changes of synthesized ME-CDs after being irradiated by a 365 nm UV lamp for different times (0 min and 150 min); inset: Digital photographs of initial and several minutes irradiated ME-CDs aqueous suspension under normal light and UV light.

Mouse fibroblast cell imaging with ME-CDs. (a) The effect of ME-CDs on mouse fibroblast cell viability (CCK8 assay), (n = 4). Student's t-test was performed for statistical analysis. ns: not significant. (b) Confocal fluorescence microscopy and bright-field image of mouse fibroblast cells incubated with ME-CDs (100 μg mL–1) for 24 and 48 h. Scale bar is 20 μm.

Human MSCs imaging with ME-CDs. (a) The effect of ME-CDs on human MSCs’ cell viability (CCK8 assay), (n = 4). Student's t-test was performed for statistical analysis. ns: not significant. (b) Confocal fluorescence microscopy and bright-field image of human MSCs incubated with ME-CDs (100 μg mL–1) for 24 and 48 h. Scale bar is 20 μm.

3D Human MSCs imaging with ME-CDs. (a) Illustration of 3D culture of HMSCs, Created with BioRender.com. (b) The effect of ME-CDs on 3D human MSCs’ cell viability (CCK8 assay), (n = 8). Student's t-test was performed for statistical analysis. ns: not significant. (c) Fluorescence microscopy and bright-field image of ME-CDs (100 μg mL–1) treated 3D human MSCs for 24 h. Scale bar is 100 μm.

Imaging of migrating cells with ME-CDs. (a) The effect of ME-CDs on mouse macrophage cell viability (CCK8 assay), (n = 6). Student's t-test was performed for statistical analysis. ns: not significant. (b) Illustrating the experimentation of macrophage's migration, Created with BioRender.com. (c) Fluorescence microscopy and bright-field image of ME-CDs (100 μg mL–1) treated mouse macrophages for 24 h. Scale bar is 20 μm.
Digital photographs of hand-drawing patterns on commercial filter paper using synthesized ME-CDs aqueous solution as a fluorescent ink under UV light.
Digital photographs of hand-drawing patterns on commercial filter paper using synthesized ME-CDs aqueous solution as a fluorescent ink under daylight.

(a) Digital photographs of hand-drawing patterns on commercial filter paper using synthesized ME-CDs aqueous solution as a fluorescent ink on the day of drawing and 150th day under daylight; (b) Digital photographs of hand-drawing patterns on commercial filter paper using synthesized ME-CDs aqueous solution as a fluorescent ink on the day of drawing and 150th day under UV light.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multifunctional tunable-emissive carbon dots (ME-CDs) were hydrothermally synthesized and characterized for their structural, morphological, and optical properties, as well as their applications in biological imaging and anti-counterfeiting. Scanning electron microscopy revealed uniformly dispersed wrinkle-shaped particles, while high-resolution transmission electron microscopy confirmed sizes below 5 nm. Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy validated the carbon-dominant composition, with functional groups essential for biological interactions. Raman spectroscopy showed a partially disordered graphite-like structure with a 0.72 D/G intensity ratio, and Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy identified surface functionalities. The UV–vis absorption spectrum exhibited characteristic π–π* and n–π* transitions at 280 nm and 340 nm, while fluorescence studies demonstrated strong excitation-dependent emission suitable for imaging. ME-CDs showed remarkable stability, maintaining fluorescence intensity over 200 days and under UV irradiation. As fluorescent ink, they displayed clear visibility under UV light and prolonged durability. Biocompatibility studies confirmed non-toxicity, enabling effective labeling of fibroblasts and human mesenchymal stem cells (HMSCs) with confocal visualization. ME-CDs also penetrated 3D HMSC spheroids, providing multicolor fluorescence without affecting cell viability, and efficiently tracked macrophage migration. The robust optical properties, biocompatibility, and durability of ME-CDs underscore their potential for long-term applications in bioimaging and anti-counterfeiting.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, significant advancements have been made in the field of nanotechnology, particularly in the synthesis and application of nanomaterials for diverse domains [1,2]. Among these, carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as a transformative class of nanomaterials due to their unique combination of biocompatibility, low toxicity, exceptional photoluminescence, and simple facile synthesis methods [3]. These properties have positioned CDs as promising candidates for diverse applications, including biomedical imaging, surveillance, and anti-counterfeiting technologies. The multifunctionality of CDs offers promising avenues for addressing diverse challenges [4]. One of the most significant strides in this area is the exploration of natural and sustainable for CD synthesis, which aligns with global initiatives toward green chemistry and environmental sustainability [5–7]. Utilizing the abundance of fruit waste and biomass as carbon precursors, researchers have developed eco-friendly synthesis methods, achieving materials with tailored optical properties, superior biocompatibility, and low environmental impact [8]. These natural precursors, rich in organic compounds such as sugars, cellulose, and polyphenols, undergo innovative carbonization and surface passivation, resulting in CDs with highly tunable photoluminescent properties and functional groups derived from their parent sources. In addition to the environmental benefits, CDs derived from fruit sources exhibit favorable properties for biomedical and environmental applications [9,10]. Such advances underscore the potential of CDs as a sustainable alternative to traditional imaging agents and functional materials, particularly for applications requiring eco-conscious solutions [11,12].

The synthesis of CDs can be achieved through various routes, each offering unique advantages and opportunities for tailoring the properties of the resulting nanomaterials. The most well-known methods include hydrothermal [13], pyrolysis [14], microwave-assisted [15], and electrochemical processes [16], each contributing to the versatility and applicability of CDs in diverse fields [17,18]. One of the most widely used methods for CD synthesis is the hydrothermal [19] or solvothermal route. This approach involves heating carbon-rich precursors in a solvent under high pressure and temperature, leading to carbonization and formation of CDs [20]. Hydrothermal synthesis is particularly interesting and attractive due to its simplicity, scalability, and ability to produce CDs with controlled size and surface functionalities [21]. In addition, this method is environmentally friendly and can utilize a wide range of natural precursors, including plant extracts, fruit juices, and biomass, aligning with sustainable and green chemistry principles [22]. In addition to their role in biological imaging, CDs are increasingly being explored for anti-counterfeiting technologies [23]. The unique optical properties of CDs, such as their size-dependent fluorescence and customizable surface chemistry, make them ideal for developing advanced security inks and markers. Besides, CDs can be incorporated into various substrates, including paper, textiles, and plastics, to provide a robust authentication mechanism. CDs can be utilized to print invisible quick response codes or barcodes that are only visible under specific lighting conditions, adding an extra layer of security to valuable products and documents [24]. By combining fluorescence with other properties like magnetism or chemical reactivity, CDs can be designed to respond to multiple stimuli, thereby enhancing the complexity and security of anti-counterfeiting measures [25,26].

2D and 3D imaging of cells using imaging agents is crucial for advancing our understanding of cellular processes and disease mechanisms. In 2D cell cultures, high-resolution imaging with carbon dots allows researchers to observe detailed cellular structures and interactions, providing foundational insights into cell biology [27]. Moving to 3D cultures, which more closely mimic in vivo conditions, CDs facilitate deep tissue imaging, helping to unravel complex cell-cell and cell-matrix interactions that are critical for understanding tissue development, cancer, and regenerative medicine [28–30]. For migratory cells, in vivo cellular tracking with CDs is essential for studying dynamic physiological and pathological processes like metastasis and immune response in various physiological and pathological environments including tumor microenvironment and inflammation [31–33]. These kinds of comprehensive approaches enhance our ability to understand pathophysiologies and to develop targeted therapies, disease modelings, and tissue engineering.

This study introduces significant advancements in the synthesis and application of multifunctional tunable-emissive carbon dots (ME-CDs) derived from unripe black mulberry fruits, showcasing an eco-friendly and sustainable approach. The hydrothermal method employed facilitates the generation of ME-CDs with high quantum yield, durable photostability, and tunable fluorescence emissions, making them ideal for advanced imaging and anti-counterfeiting applications. Notably, the ability of ME-CDs to image cells in 2D, 3D, and migratory states offers a powerful tool for investigating cellular processes, disease mechanisms, and tissue interactions, thereby propelling biomedical research. Furthermore, the integration of elemental mapping and surface morphology studies underscores the relationship between synthesis parameters and material properties, paving the way for fine-tuning optical and surface functionalities. The findings highlight how functional group distribution and elemental composition impact the photostability, fluorescence behavior, and biocompatibility of carbon dots, fostering a deeper understanding of nanoscale interactions at the material interface. The study's emphasis on tunable surface properties opens new avenues for the development of materials with improved performance in imaging, sensing, and authentication technologies, bridging sustainability and high-tech applications.

The ME-CDs were synthesized hydrothermally using black mulberry fruits. In brief, black mulberry fruit (unripe) was minced well with demineralized water using a commercial mixer-grinder. The obtained mulberry fruit solution was taken into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, which was tightly closed and then heated in a hot-air oven at 200 °C for 24 h. The Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave was cooled down to room temperature, and the hydrothermally carbonized solution was filtered through a mixed cellulose esters membrane to remove bigger particles (pore size: 0.22 μm). The resulting supernatant suspension (ME-CDs) was partially stored in a refrigerator at 4–5 °C until required. The remaining supernatant suspension was dried using a Freze dryer to get solid particles for further confirmation of the structural/morphological characterization. Details of the simple hydrothermal carbonization method used to synthesize the ME-CDs from black mulberry fruit are shown in Scheme 1 .

The surface morphology of the prepared ME-CDs via the hydrothermal technique (200 °C) was characterized using scanning electron microscopy (SEM), as shown in Fig. 1 (a–d). SEM images exhibit a regular distribution of small, wrinkle-shaped surfaces, indicating that the ME-CDs are rather evenly distributed and of a consistent size. Fig. 1(a) reveals grooves on the CDs, which can be attributed to their graphitic nature and the presence of functional groups on the surface. These grooves likely arise from the intrinsic structural properties of graphitic carbon and are further influenced by various functional groups attached to the ME-CDs [34]. This combination of graphitic grooves and surface functionalities can significantly impact the physical and chemical properties of the ME-CDs, enhancing their interactions with light and other materials. Such features may improve the optical properties of CDs, such as photoluminescence, and increase their potential for applications in areas like sensing, imaging, and catalysis [35,36]. The smooth morphology (Fig. 1(b–d)) is likely attributable to their surface functionalities and the presence of smaller particle domains. The unique wrinkle-shaped surface morphology is significant as it can affect the optical properties of the ME-CDs, potentially enhancing their photoluminescence and other light-related behavious. Moreover, these surface wrinkles may also play a crucial role in the surface chemistry of the CDs, influencing the interaction with different materials. The increased surface and distinct topography provided by the wrinkles could enhance the adsorption capabilities and catalytic activities of the ME-CDs, making them particularly effective for applications in sensing, imaging, and the development of nanocomposites. This intricate surface structure could also facilitate better dispersion and stability within various matrices, further expanding their potential uses in advanced material science and nanotechnology [37].

SEM image and the corresponding elemental mapping image have been provided to identify the elemental composition of the synthesized ME-CDs. The elemental mapping (Fig. 2 ) confirmed that carbon is the major element in the synthesized ME-CDs. The presence of oxygen and nitrogen is associated with functional groups such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups on the ME-CDs [38]. Figure S1a provides evidence of the presence of carbon, which is accompanied by significant quantities of other elements. The overlapping Energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy mapping, represented in various colors, visually identifies these different elements across the same area. However, due to limitations, a precise quantitative analysis could not be performed. The EDX spectrum (Figure S1b) confirmed the presence of three elements, namely nitrogen, carbon, and oxygen. Additionally, the Platinum (Pt) peak results due to coating over the synthesized CDs, and a Silicon (Si) peak appears as a result of ME-CDs being deposited over the Si substrate.

The particle morphology of the ME-CDs was studied using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), a flexible and effective instrument for imaging and describing nanoscale particles (Fig. 3 a–c). TEM provided high-resolution images of the synthesized ME-CDs, revealing that they are predominantly <5 nm in size. These high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) (Fig. 3d) images clearly showed that the outer surface of carbon dots possesses an amorphous structure, lacking a well-defined crystalline lattice. The absence of lattice fringes and diffraction patterns in the HR-TEM images confirmed the amorphous nature of the carbon dots. This amorphous morphology indicates a random arrangement of carbon atoms, which could be attributed to the specific synthesis conditions and the presence of various functional groups such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups, that disrupt the formation of a regular crystalline structure. The amorphous nature of the ME-CDs is significant as it can influence their optical and electronic properties, making them suitable for bioimaging, sensing, and photothermal therapy. Fig. 3d images of the carbon dots reveal bright spots that stand out against a darker background. The dense concentration of electrons in the carbon atoms within the dots is the cause of their brightness. In TEM imaging, areas with higher electron density appear brighter because they scatter more electrons. Upon closer examination of the HR-TEM images, certain regions (inner core) exhibit lattice fringes that indicate an ideal grapheneD-spacing of 0.21 nm corresponding to the (100) planes. This observation matches well with the calculated values from XRD results, confirming the presence of graphene-like structures within the amorphous carbon matrix [39]. The combination of amorphous nature and crystalline regions within the ME-CDs suggests a complex internal structure. The amorphous regions, characterized by the absence of a well-defined crystalline lattice, coexist with areas where graphene-like structures are present. These crystalline regions contribute to the unique properties of the CDs, such as enhanced electrical conductivity and specific optical behaviors [40]. Thus, the TEM analysis, supported by XRD results, provides a detailed understanding of the particle morphology of the ME-CDs.

Figure S2 presents the size distribution analysis derived from the corresponding TEM images shown in Fig. 3b. The size distribution graphs indicate that the ME-CDs exhibit a narrow size range, predominantly between 2 and 6 nm. This narrow distribution is crucial as it suggests a consistent and uniform synthesis process, leading to homogeneous particle sizes. The average size of the ME-CDs is calculated to be approximately 4 nm. It is optimal for balancing sufficient brightness with minimal cytotoxicity, making ME-CDs suitable for long-term imaging and tracking of cellular processes [41]. Additionally, the narrow size distribution minimizes variability in signal intensity, leading to cleaner and more precise imaging.

Further, in anti-counterfeiting technologies, the narrow size distribution and uniform fluorescence of ME-CDs can be utilized to create intricate and highly specific patterns that are difficult to replicate. ME-CDs can be incorporated into inks or coatings used on packaging, documents, or currency. Their consistent size and fluorescence properties allow for the generation of unique optical signatures that can be verified under specific lighting conditions, such as UV light [42].

The XRD pattern analysis (Fig. 4 a) of the synthesized ME-CDs showed significant insights into the structural characteristics of the material. A dominant peak is observed at 23°, which corresponds to the (002) lattice spacing typical of carbon-based materials. The D-spacing associated with this peak is 0.39 nm, indicating the distance between adjacent layers of carbon atoms. Given that this spacing is greater than that of highly crystalline graphite or graphene, some degree of disorder or expansion within the carbon layers may exist. The broad nature of this peak implies that the carbon structure lacks long-range order, which is characteristic of amorphous or poorly ordered materials. Additionally, a smaller peak appears at 42.5°, linked to the (100) plane in the hexagonal lattice of carbon atoms. The corresponding D-spacing for this peak is 0.21 nm, which is again indicative of an expanded or disordered structure (surface-defected sp3 carbon skeleton) compared to ideal graphite. The presence of this peak, along with its broadness, further confirms that the material has a significant degree of structural disorder. The broad peaks are indicative of a lack of well-defined, repeating crystal lattices and suggest the presence of substantial defects or variations in the stacking of the carbon layers. The overall pattern, characterized by broad and shifted peaks, provides valuable information about the degree of graphization or ordering within the ME-CDs. The data suggest that the material has a low degree of graphization, confirming it is more amorphous or disordered than highly crystalline graphite. The larger D-spacing values and the broad nature of the peaks confirm that the carbon layers are not tightly packed, indicating a partially ordered or disordered structure. This degree of disorder or expansion within the carbon layers can significantly influence the physical properties of the ME-CDs, such as their electronic, optical, and mechanical behaviors, making them suitable for multiple applications where such properties are desirable [43]. Thus, these findings from the XRD pattern align well with the results obtained from TEM. The broad peak in the XRD pattern can be attributed to the amorphous nature of the ME-CDs or their small particle size, as seen in TEM results. The consistency between XRD and TEM results strengthens the conclusion that the ME-CDs have an essentially amorphous or nanocrystalline structure with a significant degree of disorder, reinforcing their potential for various applications.

The Raman spectroscopy analysis offers valuable insights into the degree of graphitization in the synthesized ME-CDs. As depicted in Fig. 4(b), the Raman spectrum features distinct D and G bands. The D band, located around 1385 cm⁻¹, is indicative of defects and structural irregularities in the carbon framework, reflecting the presence of sp³ hybridized carbon atoms or imperfections. Meanwhile, the G band, positioned near 1585 cm⁻¹, corresponds to sp² hybridized carbon-carbon bonds, characteristic of graphitic structures. The intensity ratio of the D to G bands (Iᴰ/Iᴳ) serves as a critical metric for evaluating the balance between structural disorder and graphitization. With an Iᴰ/Iᴳ value of 0.72, the ME-CDs exhibit a partially disordered structure while retaining appreciable graphitic domains, highlighting their unique carbon architecture [44].

The deconvoluted Raman spectrum (Figure S3(a)) provided detailed information about the structural characteristics. Two primary peaks can be seen in the original spectrum: the G band is around 1585 cm–1, which corresponds to the in-plane vibration of sp2 carbon atoms and indicates graphitic structures, and the D band, about 1385 cm–1, which indicates defects and disorder within the carbon lattice. By fitting these overlapping peaks with Gaussian functions, the deconvolution method makes it possible to identify sub-peaks that represent different kinds of defects and contributions from amorphous carbon. Further, accuracy is ensured by baseline correction, and the distinct features of the ME-CDs are subsequently determined by analyzing each fitted peak separately. The cumulative (Figure S3(b)) fitted curve, the summation of all individual fitted peaks, closely matches the original spectrum, confirming the validity of the deconvolution process. This fitted curve provides a thorough understanding of the structure of the material by illustrating the contribution of each peak to the overall spectrum. The significant variables that provide information about the degree of disorder and graphitization include the area ratio (AD/AG) between the D and G bands as well as the positions and widths of the peaks. A higher AD/AG ratio suggests moderate defects, while narrower peaks indicate more ordered structures. These detailed structural insights from the Raman spectrum are essential for correlating the properties of ME-CDs with their performance in applications in bioimaging and sensing. These Raman spectroscopy results are consistent with the findings of the XRD analysis. Both techniques point to a partially disordered graphite-like structure in the ME-CDs. The combined interpretation of XRD and Raman spectroscopy results provides a comprehensive understanding of the ME-CD structure, confirming that they exhibit a mixture of amorphous and graphitic characteristics.

Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Fig. 4c) is an important analytical tool used to confirm the functional groups present in the synthesized ME-CDs preliminarily. The FTIR spectrum typically shows several characteristic absorption bands that correspond to various functional groups essential for the applications of CDs. The broadband in the region of 3100–3500 cm–1 indicates the presence of hydroxyl (O–H) and amine (N–H) groups, enhancing water dispersibility/solubility and biocompatibility, which are crucial for biological applications [45]. The C–H symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations around 2850–2950 cm–1 suggest the presence of alkyl groups, affecting the hydrophobicity of the CDs. The sharp band at 1660 cm–1 corresponds to the carbonyl (C=O) stretching vibrations, which are significant for optical properties and chemical reactivity, while the band at 1575 cm–1 indicates the presence of aromatic or conjugated structures (C=C), essential for photoluminescence. Additionally, the band at 1455 cm–1 indicates C–N stretching, pointing to nitrogen doping enhancing electronic properties. Bands in the 1350–1430 cm–1 region are associated with N–H and O–H bending, confirming their hydrophilic nature. The C–OH stretching vibrations at 1295 cm–1 ensure stability and dispersibility in aqueous solutions, and the C–O–C stretching vibrations centered at 1050 cm–1 indicate the presence of ether/epoxy groups, contributing to structural diversity [46]. In addition, the peak at 612 cm–1 corresponds to –CH bending vibrations/–CH2 out-plane stretching vibrations, providing information about the aliphatic character of the carbon framework. The presence of functional groups like hydroxyl, amine, and carbonyl groups enhances the biocompatibility and hydrophilicity of the ME-CDs, facilitating their interaction with cellular components for specific labeling and imaging [47]. The presence of aromatic and conjugated structures (C=C bands) contributes to strong fluorescence, making CDs effective imaging agents. Further, the photoluminescent properties of ME-CDs can be improved by the presence of functional groups like C=O, C–N, and C–O–C, which modify the emission properties and ensure the stability of the synthesized material [48].

Following the FTIR analysis, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to confirm the presence of these functional groups and to quantitatively assess the elemental composition and states of the prepared ME-CDs. The ME-CDs survey spectrum of XPS (Fig. 4d) showed three notable peaks at 285, 400, and 532 eV corresponding to C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s, respectively. XPS analysis revealed that the ME-CDs consist of 79 % carbon, 16 % oxygen, and 5 % nitrogen. The high carbon content indicates that the backbone of the ME-CDs is primarily carbon-based, supporting the presence of aromatic and aliphatic carbon structures, as suggested by the FTIR results [49]. The oxygen content is consistent with the presence of hydroxyl, carbonyl, and ether/epoxy groups, which are crucial for enhancing the hydrophilicity and fluorescent properties of the ME-CDs. The nitrogen content, confirmed through the C–N stretching bands in the FTIR spectrum, indicates successful nitrogen doping, which can enhance the electronic properties and quantum yield of the synthesized material. By deconvoluting the XPS peaks, detailed information about the bonding environments, such as C–C, C–N, C–O, and C=O, can be obtained, further confirming the functional groups identified by FTIR.

Further, to determine the chemical bond information, the C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s binding energy levels were deconvoluted and fitted using Casa XPS software. Fig. 5 focuses on the deconvolution of the C 1 s, O 1 s, and N 1 s regions, providing detailed information about the particular functional groups and bonding environments within the CDs. The deconvolution of the C 1 s XPS spectrum (Fig. 5a) of the synthesized ME-CDs showed five distinct peaks, each corresponding to different types of carbon bonding, providing detailed information about their surface chemistry. The peak at 284.8 eV is attributed to sp2 hybridized carbon atoms (C–C and C=C bonding), confirming the presence of graphitic or aromatic structures, which are essential for the electronic properties and conductivity of the ME-CDs. At 285.6 eV, the peak is associated with C–O bonds, indicating hydroxyl (C–OH) and ether (C–O–C) groups on the surface, which enhances the hydrophilicity and dispersibility of the ME-CDs in aqueous solutions, making them more biocompatible. The peak at 286.3 eV corresponds to carbon atoms bonded to nitrogen, indicating the presence of C–N bonds due to nitrogen doping, which enhances the quantum yield and alters the emission characteristics of the CDs. The peak at 287.5 eV corresponds to carbonyl (C=O) groups, indicating surface oxidation, providing active sites for chemical modifications, and influencing the fluorescence properties of the ME-CDs. Finally, the peak at 289.0 eV is attributed to carboxyl (HO–C=O) groups, indicating a higher degree of surface oxidation, which increases the stability and dispersibility of the ME-CDs in aqueous solutions and facilitates functionalization and conjugation with various molecules [50].

The deconvolution of the O 1 s XPS spectrum (Fig. 5b) of the synthesized ME-CDs showed three distinct peaks, each corresponding to different oxygen-containing functional groups. The first peak, centered around 531.6 eV, corresponds to carbonyl (C = O) groups. The second peak, centered at approximately 532.6 eV, corresponds to the C–O–C and C–OH bonds. This indicates the presence of ether/epoxy and hydroxyl groups on the surface of the ME-CDs. The third peak, centered at approximately 533.6 eV, is associated with carbonyl (O = C–OH) groups. These groups provide stability/dispersibility and make it easier for them to become further functional. The synthesized ME-CDs exhibited three distinct peaks in the N 1 s XPS spectrum, which are all associated with different nitrogen-containing functional groups (Fig. 5c). The first peak, which appears at about 399.3 eV, is attributed to pyridinic nitrogen, in which two carbon atoms form a hexagonal ring structure with nitrogen atoms bound to them. This structure enhances the conductivity and catalytic activities. The second peak is around 400.2 eV and corresponds to pyrrolic nitrogen, where the nitrogen atoms are part of a five-membered ring, contributing to the photoluminescent properties. The third peak at approximately 401.1 eV is associated with graphitic nitrogen, where nitrogen atoms are integrated into a hexagonal carbon lattice, significantly increasing the stability, conductivity, and electron transfer properties [51,52]. Thus, in good agreement with the FTIR data, the XPS spectra confirmed the presence of hydrophilic functional groups, including –COOH, –NH, and –OH moieties on the surfaces of the synthesized ME-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of the synthesized ME-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 6 a) of the synthesized ME-CDs provides detailed information on optical properties and electronic structure. The spectrum typically shows a strong absorption peak in the UV region, around 280–300 nm, associated with π–π* transitions of the aromatic C = C bonds present in the carbon core of the ME-CDs. This peak indicates the presence of conjugated π systems, which are essential for the optical activity of CDs. In addition, a secondary absorption peak appeared in the visible region, indicating n–π* transitions associated with the surface functional groups such as C=O [53]. Further, the images (Fig. 6a inset) of the aqueous ME-CDs under daylight and UV light (365 nm) showed their clear visual and fluorescent properties, respectively. Under daylight, the synthesized ME-CDs appeared as a clear pale yellow solution. At the same time, in UV light, they exhibited strong fluorescence (cyan-blue color emission), illustrating their potential for applications in bioimaging and anti-counterfeiting [54].

The fluorescence emission and excitation spectra of the ME-CDs (Fig. 6b) are essential for understanding their luminescent behavior. The excitation spectrum typically showed a peak that corresponds to the optimal wavelength for exciting the ME-CDs to produce maximum fluorescence emission, usually within the UV to blue region (around 365 nm). Upon excitation at this wavelength, the emission spectrum of the ME-CDs generally displays a broad peak, often centered around 433 nm, depending on the surface functional groups and synthesis method. The significant feature is the Stokes shift; the difference between the excitation and emission peaks was found to be 68 nm. This significant Stokes shift helps in reducing the reabsorption of emitted light and enhances the brightness of the fluorescence, making the ME-CDs particularly useful for bioimaging and sensing applications [55,56]. The emission spectra of fluorescence at various excitation wavelengths illustrated the tunability of their emission properties (Fig. 6c). When excited at various wavelengths (290 to 500 nm), the ME-CDs exhibited a shift in the emission peak known as excitation-dependent emission. This characteristic results from the presence of various emissive sites within the CDs, each with different energy levels [57]. In addition, the observed shifts in the emission spectra also provide detailed information about the electronic structure and the nature of the surface functional groups, which play an important role in determining the optical properties of the CDs. These findings certainly imply that the produced ME-CDs fluorescence emissions were excitation-dependent. Further, the normalized fluorescence spectra at different excitation wavelengths confirmed their consistent and stable luminescent properties (Fig. 6d). After normalizing the spectra, it becomes viable to compare the emission intensity and peak positions directly between different excitation wavelengths. The emission spectra retained their homogeneous shape and relative intensity irrespective of variations in excitation, indicating that the ME-CDs exhibited stable and consistent emission properties. This stability and uniformity are necessary for applications that require consistent fluorescence output, such as quantitative bioimaging and reliable anti-counterfeiting measures. Further, quantum yield (QY) measurements can be utilized to understand the photoluminescent properties of CDs. The QY of the prepared ME-CDs was found to be 24 %, using quinine sulfate as a reference standard. This value reflects the efficiency with which the ME-CDs convert absorbed light into emitted fluorescence [58]. QY is an important parameter for evaluating the performance of fluorescent materials, especially in the field of bioimaging and sensing, where high fluorescence efficiency is desirable [59]. The normalized spectra indicated the potential for application in cutting-edge optical devices that require precise control over emission properties. Thus, the UV–vis absorption spectrum, fluorescence emission and excitation spectra, and the emission behavior at different excitation wavelengths collectively showed the excellent optical performance, stability, and versatility of the synthesized ME-CDs.

Similar studies on carbon dots (CDs) synthesized from bitter orange juice via a green hydrothermal method highlight their pH-dependent fluorescence, biocompatibility, and potential for bioimaging applications [60]. Similarly, the developed ME-CDs synthesized through a hydrothermal process demonstrate robust optical properties, biocompatibility, and stability. While the orange juice-derived CDs achieved a quantum yield of 19.9 % and effective cell imaging, whereas the synthesized ME-CDs exhibited excitation-dependent fluorescence, a long fluorescence lifetime, and superior photostability over 200 days, making them highly versatile for bioimaging, anti-counterfeiting, and advanced biomedical applications. Both studies emphasize eco-friendly synthesis and functional versatility in biomedical contexts.

Long-term stability refers to the ability of the CDs to maintain their structural integrity and functional properties over extended periods. This is essential for applications such as bioimaging, sensors, and anti-counterfeiting, where reliable performance is required over months or even years. The prepared ME-CDs should be sealed in airtight containers to avoid evaporation or contamination. ME-CDs showed remarkable stability in fluorescence intensity over prolonged periods of time, making them appropriate for various applications. To investigate their long-term stability, 200 days of fluorescence intensity tests were conducted. Remarkably, there were no significant changes in fluorescence intensity from day 1 to day 200 (Fig. 7 a), confirming that the developed ME-CDs maintained their optical properties over time. This stability was visually confirmed through digital photographs of the ME-CDs aqueous suspension under both normal and UV light (Fig. 7a inset image), which showed no aggregation and no visible degradation. Further, the photostability of the prepared ME-CDs was analyzed by exposing ME-CDs to continuous UV irradiation at 365 nm for different durations from 0 to 150 min (Fig. 7b). Even after continuous exposure to UV light, the results showed no significant changes in fluorescence intensity after 150 min. Digital photographs (Fig. 7b inset image) taken before and after UV irradiation showed that the ME-CDs aqueous solution retained its fluorescence under both normal light and UV light, confirming their resistance to photobleaching. Photobleaching refers to the gradual loss of fluorescence intensity due to prolonged exposure to excitation light, which can lead to the degradation of fluorescent molecules. This photostability is essential for applications that involve prolonged exposure to light, ensuring that the ME-CDs can withstand intense light conditions without losing their optical properties. Further, Figure S4 illustrates the fluorescence-normalized spectra of the synthesized ME-CDs under different conditions. Figure S4 (a) shows the spectra at day 1 and after 200 days of storage, indicating minimal or no changes and high stability over time. Figure S4 (b) depicts the spectra before and after 150 min of continuous irradiation with a 365 nm UV lamp, demonstrating the exceptional photostability of the ME-CDs with negligible shift/loss in fluorescence behavior. These results confirm the long-term stability and robustness of the ME-CDs. The exceptional stability and strong fluorescence of the hydrothermally synthesized ME-CDs make them highly effective for biocellular imaging [61]. Further, the presence of hydrophilic functional groups such as –COOH, –OH, and –NH, confirmed through FTIR and XPS analyses, showed good dispersibility in aqueous environments and compatibility with biological systems. In addition, the presence of the functional groups also facilitates the conjugation of the ME-CDs with biomolecules like antibodies, peptides, or other targeting ligands, enabling specific labeling and imaging of cellular structures [62]. Thus, the long-term live cell imaging research and tracking of dynamic biological processes benefit significantly from the fluorescence's long-term stability and resistance to photobleaching.

Fibroblasts are essential cells found in connective tissue that play crucial roles in maintaining tissue structure, repairing wounds, and regulating the immune response. Fibroblasts are responsible for producing and maintaining the extracellular matrix, which provides structural support to tissues and organs [63,64]. Imaging of fibroblasts allows researchers to visualize fibroblasts in real time and study their morphology, behavior, and interactions with other cells in various healthy and pathologic conditions, including fibrosis, arthritis, and cancer [65]. For mouse fibroblast cells, the cell counting kit-8 (CCK8) assay results showed that relative cell viability remained at >99 % after 24 h of exposure to ME-CDs at a concentration of 75 μg mL–1. Cell viability decreased by only <1.5 % when the ME-CDs concentration was increased to 100 μg mL–1. These findings indicate that ME-CDs do not exhibit significant toxicity at concentrations up to 100 μg mL–1 over a 24-hour incubation period. Similarly, relative cell viability was more than 99 % after 48 h of exposure to ME-CDs at 100 μg mL–1 (Fig. 8 a). Therefore, ME-CDs derived from natural sources could be potentially safe for in vitro imaging applications. Endocytosis is the primary pathway for CD internalization, where cells engulf the CDs through various endocytic routes such as clathrin-mediated endocytosis, caveolae-mediated endocytosis, or macropinocytosis. Once internalized, CDs are enclosed within endosomes, which then undergo maturation and fusion with lysosomes, leading to the potential release of CDs into the cytoplasm. Understanding these internalization pathways is crucial for optimizing the design of CDs for specific biomedical applications, ensuring efficient delivery and minimal cytotoxicity [66,67].

In this context, the in vitro cellular internalization of ME-CDs by mouse fibroblast cells was conducted for imaging purposes. The fibroblasts were treated with ME-CDs at a safe concentration of 100 μg mL–1, and the imaging results were captured using a confocal fluorescence microscope. The fluorescence images of ME-CDs treated cells clearly show that the ME-CDs were capable of labeling the mouse fibroblasts at 24 and 48 h. All colors were pleasantly visible in the cells under a confocal microscope. Untreated cells showed no color emission. The confocal microscope confirmed that ME-CDs easily penetrate mouse fibroblast cell membranes without causing significant morphological damage (Fig. 8b). Human mesenchymal stem cells (HMSCs) are multipotent cells found in various tissues such as bone marrow, adipose tissue, and umbilical cord blood. HMSCs hold great promise in regenerative medicine and can potentially repair and replace damaged tissues and organs, making them valuable for treating conditions such as bone fractures, cartilage defects, cerebrovascular and cardiovascular diseases [68–70]. Cellular imaging may play a crucial role in understanding the behavior and therapeutic potential of HMSCs. In HMSCs, the CCK8 assay results showed that relative cell viability remained at >99 % after 24 h of exposure to ME-CDs at a concentration of 75 μg mL–1. Cell viability decreased by only <2.5 % when the ME-CDs concentration was increased to 100 μg mL–1. These findings indicate that ME-CDs do not exhibit significant toxicity at concentrations up to 100 μg mL–1 over a 24-hour incubation period. Similarly, relative cell viability was more than 98 % after 48 h of exposure to ME-CDs at 100 μg mL–1 (Fig. 9 a). In this context, the in vitro cellular internalization of ME-CDs by human MSCs was conducted for imaging purposes. ME-CDs were also treated at the concentration of 100 μg mL–1, and the imaging results were captured as mentioned above. The fluorescence images of ME-CDs treated cells clearly show that the ME-CDs were capable of labeling the cells at 24 and 48 h. All colors were pleasantly visible also in the HMSCs under a confocal microscope, similar to that of mouse fibroblasts. Untreated cells showed no color emission. Similarly, ME-CDs easily penetrate HMSC membranes without causing significant morphological damage (Fig. 9b). The internalization of ME-CDs by the mouse fibroblast and HMSCs did not appear to negatively impact their viability, as indicated by cell viability assay and morphology results, which agree with previous studies [71–73]. Positively charged or functionalized CDs can facilitate stronger interactions with the negatively charged cell membrane, promoting uptake. These findings suggest that low cytotoxicity and biocompatible ME-CDs have promising applications in the cell labeling field.

3D Spheroids are widely used for various physiological and pathological studies, including assessing the efficacy and toxicity of pharmaceutical compounds. Cellular imaging techniques enable researchers to monitor spheroid growth, viability, and drug penetration, providing insights into how drugs interact with cells in a 3D context [74–76]. In this study, we used a U-shaped 96-well plate to make 3D spheroids, as U-shaped 96-well plates promote 3D spheroid formation by minimizing cell attachment to the surface, encouraging cells to cluster in the center. The low-attachment surface and gravity guide cells to aggregate at the well's bottom, forming a 3D structure. CCK-8 assays demonstrated the outstanding biocompatibility of ME-CDs. HMSCs cultured as 3D spheroids remained highly viable after 24 h at 37 °C, even at the concentration of 100 μg mL–1, 3D spheroid cell viability exceeded 98.6 %, underscoring the excellent biocompatibility (Fig. 10 a and b). Fluorescent microscope images of ME-CDs treated 3D HMSCs at 24 h after revealed clear multicolor fluorescence signals (blue, green, and red) within the 3D spheroid, indicating significant uptake in the 3D spheroids. ME-CDs were distributed throughout the entire 3D spheroid structure, including the periphery and inner core, confirming the successful penetration of ME-CDs into HMSCs 3D spheroids. Conversely, 3D spheroids without ME-CDs exhibited no detectable fluorescence signals within the cells (Fig. 10c). ME-CDs demonstrate exceptional biocompatibility and efficient penetration in 3D HMSC spheroids, as evidenced by high cell viability and extensive multicolor fluorescence uptake. The internalization of ME-CDs by the HMSCs 3D spheroids did not appear to negatively impact their viability, as indicated by cell viability assay and morphology results, which agree with our previous study [73]. The study on FA-conjugated green CDs demonstrates their targeted cancer detection capabilities through pH-responsive fluorescence and FR-mediated endocytosis. Similarly, ME-CDs exhibit outstanding biocompatibility and optical properties, making them effective for bioimaging in both 2D and 3D cellular environments. While the FA-CDs target specific cancer cells for early diagnosis, whereas ME-CDs focus on broad applications such as anti-counterfeiting and imaging. Both studies highlight the versatility and potential of CDs for biomedical advancements [77].

Macrophages play a crucial role in the immune system, functioning as key regulators of inflammation, tissue repair, and immune response modulation. Their ability to migrate to sites of infection, injury, or inflammation is essential for their functions [64,78–80]. Imaging techniques that allow for the visualization and tracking of macrophage migration are therefore critical for understanding various physiological and pathological processes. Here, first we tested the CCK8 assay in mouse macrophages treated with 0–100 μg mL–1 of ME-CDs, and results showed that relative cell viability remained the same after 24 h of exposure to ME-CDs at a concentration of 100 μg mL–1. These findings indicate that ME-CDs do not exhibit significant toxicity at concentrations up to 100 μg mL–1 over a 24-hour, underscoring the excellent biocompatibility (Fig. 11 a). ME-CDs (100 μg mL–1) treated macrophages in transwell migration assay showed that macrophages were migrated to other side of the transwell membrane, which was revealed by the multicolor fluorescence signals (blue, green, and red) within migratory cells. This result suggests that migratory cells still retained the ME-CDs in their compartment, and no signal was seen in any ME-CDs treated cells (Fig. 11b and c). ME-CDs at 100 μg mL–1 did not affect the macrophage migration across a transwell membrane, with retained fluorescence signals suggesting continued presence within migratory cells. These results indicate that the particles remained within the migratory cells and did not affect their migration, which can be used for the application of monitoring macrophages in vitro assay and also in vivo live cell imaging of macrophages in infection, injury, or inflammation and tumor microenvironment [79–83].

Future research on ME-CDs for cellular imaging could focus on enhancing specificity, fluorescence stability, and targeting capabilities in complex biological environments, such as tumors [84]. Investigating the interactions of ME-CDs with diverse cell types and conditions, especially in dynamic or in vivo systems, could reveal valuable insights into cellular processes such as differentiation, migration, and cellular repair mechanisms in tissue microenvironments [85] and [86]. Furthermore, advancing the multicolor imaging capacity and photostability of ME-CDs could allow extended tracking in live cell imaging, facilitating real-time monitoring of cellular responses in 3D models and organoids [87]. Future studies should also explore the long-term biocompatibility and clearance pathways of ME-CDs, especially for potential clinical applications, ensuring safety and efficacy with imaging.

To study the practical application of the synthesized product (ME-CDs), an aqueous solution of these ME-CDs was used as fluorescent ink to create hand-drawn patterns on commercial filter paper (Fig. 12 ). The ME-CDs solution was prepared by dispersing the synthesized CDs in water, forming a stable colloidal solution. The concentration range of ME-CDs in the ink formulation was typically between 100 and 200 μg mL–1, as this range provided an adequate balance between fluorescence brightness and ink viscosity for easy application. This solution was then loaded into an ink pen, allowing for precise and controlled drawing on the filter paper. Various patterns, such as intricate designs or symbols, were drawn to highlight the versatility and potential uses of ME-CDs in creative and security applications. When the filter paper with hand-drawn patterns was exposed to UV light, the fluorescent properties of the synthesized ME-CDs were clearly displayed. The excellent quantum yield and photostability of the ME-CDs are shown by their fluorescence under UV light, indicating that they are suitable for usage as fluorescent inks. In addition, the vibrant fluorescence makes ME-CDs an excellent choice for artistic and decorative purposes, providing an innovative way for artists and designers [88].

The study on CDs derived from tangerine juice highlights their use in marking cotton fibers with enhanced fluorescence for anti-counterfeiting in textiles. In comparison, the ME-CDs demonstrated broader utility, including bioimaging and anti-counterfeiting. Both studies emphasize the commercial potential of eco-friendly CDs in security and imaging applications [89]. The research of Darani et al. showed that CDs embedded into electrodeposited coatings highlight their transformative potential in corrosion-resistant technologies, emphasizing their industrial applicability. Thus, both studies underscore the significant role of carbon dots in advancing material science for diverse practical solutions [90].

Further, to illustrate the application of the synthesized ME-CDs as fluorescent ink, digital photographs of hand-drawn patterns on commercial filter paper using an aqueous solution of ME-CDs captured under daylight conditions are shown in Fig. 13 . When visualized under daylight, the patterns drawn are only slightly visible, relying on the viewer's close inspection to discern them. This property makes the ME-CDs ideal for applications where invisible or low-visibility markings are desirable until exposed to specific lighting conditions. These unique characteristics are particularly useful in anti-counterfeiting and document security, where hidden patterns can be verified under UV light without being noticeable in regular light conditions [91].

In order to study the long-term stability of the synthesized ME-CDs as fluorescent ink, digital photographs of hand-drawn patterns on commercial filter paper were taken on the day of drawing and the 150th day (Fig. 14 ). Digital photographs were taken under daylight on the hand-drawn patterns on commercial filter paper using an aqueous solution of synthesized ME-CDs as fluorescent ink, both on the day of drawing and the 150th day (Fig. 14a). On the day of drawing, the patterns appeared slightly glassy under daylight, indicating the faintness of the ME-CDs ink. By the end of the 150th day, the photographs showed no noticeable degradation, confirming the stability of the ME-CDs ink over time. The digital photographs of the same hand-drawn patterns were also taken under UV light on the day of drawing and the 150th day (Fig. 14b). On the day of drawing, the patterns were illuminated under UV light. This fluorescence highlights the high quantum yield and photostability of the ME-CDs, making them suitable for anti-counterfeiting and decorative purposes [92]. Upon the 150th day, the photographs revealed that the patterns continued to exhibit strong fluorescence under UV light, with no noticeable loss in intensity. This stability indicates that the ME-CDs maintain their fluorescent properties over an extended period of time, confirming their effectiveness for long-term use in security markings and as reliable fluorescent ink [93].

In this study, eco-friendly ME-CDs were synthesized via a simple hydrothermal method and characterized to explore their potential in bioimaging, cell migration, and anti-counterfeiting applications. SEM revealed uniformly distributed, wrinkle-shaped particles, while EDX mapping confirmed carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen as key elements. TEM showed ME-CDs primarily in the size range of 2–6 nm. XRD and Raman spectroscopy indicated a predominantly amorphous structure with partial graphite-like order, supported by a 0.72 D/G band intensity ratio. FTIR and XPS analyses identified functional groups like C–C/C=C, C–N, and C=O, essential for optical and biological applications. UV–vis absorption and fluorescence studies demonstrated excitation-dependent behavior with emission peaks between 400 and 533 nm. ME-CDs exhibited outstanding photostability, retaining over 97 % fluorescence after 200 days and 98 % under continuous UV exposure for 150 min. Biocompatibility studies confirmed high cell viability in fibroblasts, HMSCs, and macrophages up to 100 μg mL⁻¹. Confocal microscopy highlighted efficient cellular uptake and labeling in 2D and 3D cultures without affecting cell morphology or migration. ME-CDs also served as stable fluorescent inks, showing bright UV fluorescence in hand-drawn patterns even after 150 days. These properties highlight their robust optical performance, stability, and safety, making them suitable for advanced imaging and anti-counterfeiting. However, certain limitations remain. Long-term biocompatibility and in vivo evaluations are yet to be conducted to establish the safety and efficacy of ME-CDs for clinical use. Additionally, the industrial-scale production of ME-CDs requires further optimization to ensure cost-effectiveness and consistency. Addressing these limitations in future studies will enhance the practical utility and translational potential of the developed ME-CDs.
